YOUTH ACTIVISM - YOUNG PERSON CREATING CHANGE
Sources Compilation for BookBridge ESL Content
================================================

STORY THEME: Young person (teen/young adult) creates positive change in their community through activism, organizing, or leadership

SOURCES SUMMARY:
- 4 sources total (combining Marley Dias sources)
- 3 distinct narratives
- Ratings range: 7.5 - 9.5/10

================================================================================

SOURCE #1: GRETA THUNBERG - Climate Activist (FLAGSHIP)
Rating: 9.5/10
Source: The Guardian (Feature Interview)
URL: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/mar/11/greta-thunberg-schoolgirl-climate-change-warrior-some-people-can-let-things-go-i-cant

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

THE STORY:
At 15, Greta Thunberg was a painfully introverted girl with Asperger's who had fallen into deep depression after learning about climate change. She couldn't let it go like other kids could. One day in August 2018, she sat alone outside the Swedish parliament with a hand-painted sign. Her parents tried to dissuade her. Classmates declined to join. But she kept going - every Friday, in rain, sun, ice, and snow. Eight months later, she had sparked a global movement with millions of young people striking for climate action.

THE LONELY BEGINNING:

Greta Thunberg cut a frail and lonely figure when she started a school strike for the climate outside the Swedish parliament building last August. Her parents tried to dissuade her. Classmates declined to join. Passersby expressed pity and bemusement at the sight of the then unknown 15-year-old sitting on the cobblestones with a hand-painted banner.

THE GIRL WHO COULDN'T LET GO:

"I have always been that girl in the back who doesn't say anything. I thought I couldn't make a difference because I was too small."

"I overthink. Some people can just let things go, but I can't, especially if there's something that worries me or makes me sad. I remember when I was younger, and in school, our teachers showed us films of plastic in the ocean, starving polar bears and so on. I cried through all the movies. My classmates were concerned when they watched the film, but when it stopped, they started thinking about other things. I couldn't do that. Those pictures were stuck in my head."

THE DEPRESSION:

At about the age of eight, when she first learned about climate change, she was shocked that adults did not appear to be taking the issue seriously. It was not the only reason she became depressed a few years later, but it was a significant factor.

"I kept thinking about it and I just wondered if I am going to have a future. And I kept that to myself because I'm not very much of a talker, and that wasn't healthy. I became very depressed and stopped going to school."

TELLING HER PARENTS:

"When I was home, my parents took care of me, and we started talking because we had nothing else to do. And then I told them about my worries and concerns about the climate crisis and the environment. And it felt good to just get that off my chest.

They just told me everything will be all right. That didn't help, of course, but it was good to talk. And then I kept on going, talking about this all the time and showing my parents pictures, graphs and films, articles and reports. And, after a while, they started listening to what I actually said. That's when I kind of realised I could make a difference."

FINDING HER WAY OUT:

"And how I got out of that depression was that I thought: it is just a waste of time feeling this way because I can do so much good with my life. I am trying to do that still now."

CHANGING HER FAMILY FIRST:

Her parents were the guinea pigs. She discovered she had remarkable powers of persuasion, and her mother gave up flying, which had a severe impact on her career. Her father became a vegetarian.

"Over the years, I ran out of arguments," says her father. "She kept showing us documentaries, and we read books together. Before that, I really didn't have a clue. I thought we had the climate issue sorted. She changed us and now she is changing a great many other people. There was no hint of this in her childhood. It's unbelievable. If this can happen, anything can happen."

DAY ONE - AUGUST 20, 2018:

"I painted the sign on a piece of wood and, for the flyers, wrote down some facts I thought everyone should know. And then I took my bike to the parliament and just sat there. The first day, I sat alone from about 8.30am to 3pm – the regular schoolday. And then on the second day, people started joining me. After that, there were people there all the time."

OVERCOMING SELECTIVE MUTISM:

She kept her promise to strike every day until the Swedish national elections. Afterwards, she agreed to make a speech in front of thousands of people at a People's Climate March rally. Her parents were reluctant. Knowing Thunberg had been so reticent that she had previously been diagnosed with selective mutism, they tried to talk her out of it. But the teenager was determined.

"In some cases where I am really passionate, I will not change my mind."

Despite her family's concerns, she delivered the address in nearly flawless English, and invited the crowd to film her on their mobile phones and spread the message through social media. "I cried," says her proud dad.

HER ASPERGER'S AS A GIFT:

"It's nothing that I want to change about me. It's just who I am. If I had been just like everyone else and been social, then I would have just tried to start an organisation. But I couldn't do that. I'm not very good with people, so I did something myself instead."

THE TRANSFORMATION:

And now that she is active on climate, she is no longer lonely, no longer silent, no longer so depressed. She is too busy trying to make a difference. And enjoying herself.

"It's going to be very, very big internationally, with hundreds of thousands of children going to strike from school to say that we aren't going to accept this any more. I think we are only seeing the beginning. I think that change is on the horizon and the people will stand up for their future."

And then the activist slips back into being a teenager. "I'm looking forward to it and to see all the pictures the day afterwards. It's going to be fun."

KEY QUOTES FOR ESL CONTENT:
---------------------------
• "I have always been that girl in the back who doesn't say anything. I thought I couldn't make a difference because I was too small."
• "Those pictures were stuck in my head."
• "I kept thinking about it and I just wondered if I am going to have a future."
• "How I got out of that depression was that I thought: it is just a waste of time feeling this way because I can do so much good with my life."
• "The first day, I sat alone from about 8.30am to 3pm."
• "I cried," says her proud dad.
• "And now that she is active on climate, she is no longer lonely, no longer silent, no longer so depressed."

ESL MULTIPLIERS:
----------------
✅ Communication Barriers (selective mutism, Asperger's, "not very good with people")
✅ "Not Good Enough" / Too Small ("I thought I couldn't make a difference because I was too small")
✅ First-Time Courage (first strike alone, first public speech)
✅ Persistence Despite Setbacks (every Friday in rain, sun, ice, snow)
✅ Belonging/Identity (loner → movement leader, Asperger's as "blessing")
✅ Learning Journey (teaching her parents, changing their minds)

WHY THIS WORKS FOR ESL:
- Exceptional emotional arc (depression → action → global movement)
- 7+ identifiable emotional moments
- Universal theme: One small person can make a difference
- Specific sensory details (hand-painted sign, bike to parliament, cobblestones)
- Father's perspective adds emotional depth


================================================================================

SOURCE #2: MARLEY DIAS - #1000BlackGirlBooks (COMBINED SOURCES)
Rating: 8/10 (combined)
Sources: Atlas of the Future + Literary Hub
URLs: 
- https://atlasofthefuture.org/futurehero-marley-dias-mini-literary-activist/
- https://lithub.com/fourteen-year-old-marley-dias-tireless-promoter-of-diversity-in-literature/

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

THE STORY:
At 11 years old, Marley Dias was frustrated. Every book assigned in her fifth grade class featured "white boys and their dogs" - Where the Red Fern Grows, Old Yeller, Shiloh. Five books in one year, none with characters who looked like her. Instead of just complaining, she launched #1000BlackGirlBooks, a campaign to collect and donate 1,000 books featuring Black girls as main characters. She exceeded her goal, collecting over 12,000 books from around the world, and wrote her own book to help other kids become activists.

THE FRUSTRATION THAT SPARKED IT ALL:

"It was reading Where the Red Fern Grows by Wilson Rawls, which was the last book I read in fifth grade. I'd also read Old Yeller and the Shiloh series [in class]. So that was five books where white boys and their dogs were the main characters, and that was in one year. I noticed that pattern was happening a lot, and I became frustrated, because as much as I like those stories and I liked my teacher, I wanted to see myself and I wanted to see other people and their experiences."

THE OPTIONS SHE CONSIDERED:

"Option 1: focus on me, get myself more books; have my dad take me to Barnes and Noble and just be done, live my perfect life in suburban New Jersey. Option 2: find some authors, beg them to write more black girl books so I'd have some of my own, special editions, treat myself a bit."

She chose Option 3: Change the system.

HER MOTHER'S INFLUENCE:

"My mother was born and raised in Jamaica. I chose Saint Mary, Jamaica as the first 1000 black girl books donation point as I wanted to experience my mother's history. She came to America when she was 13 years old and I thought that it would be important to create the full circle."

LEARNING ABOUT ENTITLEMENT:

"The word that sums up the most challenging thing about the #1000blackgirlsbooks project is 'entitlement.' At first when I realised that I was able to help people I felt like a charity. I thought 'I'm giving the books to you, you should be grateful, you should all hail Marley.' That's not really something I want to embrace."

STAYING GROUNDED:

"I don't want my friends to change their perception of me. I sometimes don't tell my friends what I'm up to out of school. I don't lie to them, I just say, I'm going to work or my mum and I need to do something."

DEFYING GENDER EXPECTATIONS:

"I was a quarterback on my football team. All my friends were like 'that's a boy thing, you could have signed up for cheerleading.' I never say things are a boy colour or a girl colour."

THE CHARACTER SHE RELATES TO:

"The character I most relate to is in Jacqueline Woodson's 'Brown Girl Dreaming'. It's about a girl who loves to read. She just loves her family. Like me, she doesn't have any pets and she loves to write, poetry and school."

HER MESSAGE TO OTHER KIDS:

"I would like to tell the girls who don't think they can make a difference… that whoever put that idea in your head you should tell them to stop. You can, and as long as you firmly believe in whatever you're saying your dreams and wishes will come true."

"Frustration is fuel that can lead to the development of an innovative and useful idea."

"To take that first step of bravery is to really focus on finding your passions and not think that you have to wait to be great."

THE IMPACT:

- Collected over 12,000 books (goal was 1,000)
- Books donated to Jamaica, Ghana, and communities across the U.S.
- Spoke at White House alongside Michelle Obama and Oprah Winfrey
- Named to Forbes' 30 Under 30 (youngest member ever)
- Wrote her own book: "Marley Dias Gets It Done: And So Can You!"

KEY QUOTES FOR ESL CONTENT:
---------------------------
• "I wanted to see myself and I wanted to see other people and their experiences."
• "At first when I realised that I was able to help people I felt like a charity. I thought 'I'm giving the books to you, you should be grateful, you should all hail Marley.' That's not really something I want to embrace."
• "I don't want my friends to change their perception of me."
• "Whoever put that idea in your head that you can't make a difference, you should tell them to stop."
• "Frustration is fuel that can lead to the development of an innovative and useful idea."

ESL MULTIPLIERS:
----------------
✅ Belonging/Identity (wanting to see herself in books)
✅ "Not Good Enough" (kids thinking they can't make a difference)
✅ First-Time Courage (starting a national campaign at 11)
✅ Learning Journey (learning about entitlement, staying humble)
✅ Connection Across Differences (Jamaica heritage, diversity focus)

WHY THIS WORKS FOR ESL:
- Relatable frustration (not seeing yourself represented)
- Clear action from frustration
- Self-awareness about personal growth (entitlement lesson)
- Kid-friendly voice and perspective
- Named after Bob Marley - cultural connection


================================================================================

SOURCE #3: MARI COPENY "LITTLE MISS FLINT" - Water Crisis Activist
Rating: 7.5/10
Source: Metro Times
URL: https://www.metrotimes.com/detroit-guides/as-little-miss-flint-amariyanna-mari-copeny-continues-to-shine-a-light-on-the-water-crisis-23423416/

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

THE STORY:
At 8 years old, Mari Copeny watched her mother cover her sister in ointment and plastic wrap because of mysterious rashes - not knowing the tap water was poisoned with lead. When she learned about the Flint water crisis, she wrote a letter to President Obama, inviting him to visit her city. To everyone's surprise, Obama wrote back and came to Flint. Mari became "Little Miss Flint," a national voice for clean water, and continues to advocate for communities with contaminated water across America.

THE WATER CRISIS HITS HOME:

In 2016, Mari, then 8 years old, watched as her mother covered her sister Keilani in an ointment and plastic wrap because she was breaking out in mysterious rashes. What they didn't know is that the tap water flowing from their own faucets was contaminated with lead and other toxic chemicals.

"We didn't know what was causing it," her mother Brezzell says. "We thought it was the detergent or something. Then, we found out it was from the water."

THE LETTER TO PRESIDENT OBAMA:

Once the crisis was exposed, Mari wrote a letter to then-President Barack Obama before embarking on a bus ride to Washington to watch then-Gov. Rick Snyder testify about Flint before Congress.

Mari's letter: "Mr. President, hello my name is Mari Copeny, and I'm 8 years old, I live in Flint, Michigan... I am one of the children that is affected by this water and I've been doing my best to march in protest and to speak out for all the kids that live here in Flint."

"My mom said chances are you will be too busy with more important things, but there is a lot of people coming on these buses and even just a meeting from you or your wife would really lift people's spirits."

OBAMA'S RESPONSE:

Obama announced his decision to visit Flint by posting his response letter to Mari.

"You're right that Presidents are often busy, but the truth is, in America, there is no more important title than citizen. Letters from kids like you are what make me so optimistic for the future."

BEFORE THE WATER CRISIS:

Before becoming one of the many faces of the Flint water crisis, Mari was a beauty pageant contestant, which is how she earned her nickname, "Little Miss Flint."

Mari says she doesn't remember much from that period in her life, and frankly doesn't like reminiscing on it. "All of the moms complained about how their kid didn't win. I'm kind of too old to be wearing a crown and sash around, traveling around. That's just a little bit baby-ish."

GIVING AWAY HER OWN FILTER:

Water filtration company owner Eric Roy "did a fundraiser to send filters here, and he sent us one of his filters initially." But the family ended up giving it away to an elderly neighbor who needed it more.

STILL WORKING:

Now 12, Mari has expanded her advocacy work beyond the city of Flint into communities like Newark, New Jersey, east Chicago, and Pittsburgh, where water has also been contaminated.

At a recent holiday drive, when asked for an interview, Mari told her sister and aunt she didn't want to talk to a reporter at that moment. She'd rather be helping set up the toy drive. (She eventually agreed to the interview.)

KEY QUOTES FOR ESL CONTENT:
---------------------------
• "My mom said chances are you will be too busy with more important things, but there is a lot of people coming on these buses and even just a meeting from you or your wife would really lift people's spirits."
• Obama: "You're right that Presidents are often busy, but the truth is, in America, there is no more important title than citizen."
• "We didn't know what was causing it. We thought it was the detergent or something. Then, we found out it was from the water."
• "One girl can change the world, even big or small!"

ESL MULTIPLIERS:
----------------
✅ First-Time Courage (8-year-old writing to the President)
✅ Persistence Despite Setbacks (water crisis ongoing for years)
✅ Communication Barriers (child's voice being heard by powerful adults)
✅ Connection Across Differences (helping elderly neighbor, other cities)

WHY THIS WORKS FOR ESL:
- Child's voice being heard by the most powerful person in the country
- Family crisis makes it personal and emotional
- Simple, clear action (write a letter)
- Shows character (gave away filter, prefers helping to interviews)
- Ongoing relevance (water crises continue)


================================================================================

COMPILATION NOTES FOR BOOKBRIDGE
================================

THEME SUMMARY: Youth Activism - Young Person Creating Change

FLAGSHIP STORY (Use as primary content):
1. Greta Thunberg - Depression to global movement, one girl sitting alone

STRONG SUPPORTING STORIES:
2. Marley Dias - "White boys and their dogs" frustration to 12,000 books
3. Mari Copeny - 8-year-old's letter brings President to Flint

EMOTIONAL ARCS REPRESENTED:
• Lonely outsider → movement leader (Greta)
• Personal frustration → systemic change (Marley)
• Family crisis → national advocacy (Mari)
• Depression → purpose (Greta)
• Entitlement → humility (Marley)

KEY UNIVERSAL MESSAGES:
• "I thought I couldn't make a difference because I was too small"
• One person can spark a movement
• Frustration is fuel for change
• Your voice matters, even as a child
• You don't have to wait to be great

DIVERSITY OF NARRATIVES:
• Gender: All female activists
• Age at start: 8, 11, 15
• Geography: Sweden, USA (New Jersey, Michigan)
• Issues: Climate change, representation in books, clean water
• Backgrounds: Asperger's/selective mutism, Black American, working-class

AGE-APPROPRIATE THEMES:
• Feeling different/like an outsider
• Adults not listening
• Wanting to see yourself represented
• Family support and influence
• Small actions leading to big change

VOCABULARY CONSIDERATIONS FOR ESL:
- Greta: "cobblestones," "selective mutism," "Asperger's," "reticent," "cognitive dissonance"
- Marley: "protagonist," "diversity," "entitlement," "revolutionary"
- Mari: "contaminated," "infrastructure," "advocate," "crisis"

SUGGESTED SIMPLIFICATION APPROACH:
- Greta's story has the most complex vocabulary but clearest emotional arc
- Marley's story is most accessible language-wise
- Mari's story has clear cause-and-effect structure

================================================================================
END OF COMPILATION
================================================================================
